summary:
The Kassite dynasty ruled Babylonia from approximately 1595 to 1155 BC, making it the longest-reigning dynasty in Babylonian history at roughly four and a half centuries. The Kassites were a people of uncertain origin — possibly from the Zagros Mountains to the northeast — who had been settling in Babylon as mercenaries and laborers for generations before seizing power in the wake of a Hittite raid that ended the [[event:52|First Babylonian dynasty]].

The Kassite period has traditionally been viewed as a cultural dark age, but modern scholarship presents a more nuanced picture. The Kassites assimilated rapidly into Babylonian culture, adopting the Akkadian language and cuneiform writing, patronizing the scribal schools, and worshipping the traditional Babylonian gods. They added the cult of their own deities to the Babylonian pantheon. Kassite kings undertook significant restoration of damaged temples and sponsored the editing and standardization of Babylonian literary and scholarly traditions — the canonical versions of the Epic of Gilgamesh, for example, reached something like their final form during this period.

The Kassite period saw Babylonia participate actively in the Late Bronze Age international system, exchanging embassies and gifts with Egypt, Assyria, and the Hittites. The [[event:1804|Amarna Letters]] include extensive Kassite-Egyptian diplomatic correspondence, demonstrating that Babylonian kings were treated as equals by the Egyptian pharaohs. Kassite kings developed the kudurru — decorated boundary stones recording royal land grants — as a distinctive administrative and religious art form.

The dynasty ended around 1155 BC when Elamite invasions from southwestern Iran sacked Babylon and carried off the statue of the city god Marduk, along with the stele of [[event:1828|Hammurabi's Code]]. Despite its length, the Kassite dynasty is one of the less studied of Babylonian ruling houses, partly because Babylon itself lies beneath the modern water table and has been difficult to excavate.
